text: This Act may be called the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898, and it extends to the whole of Pakistan.
Definitions include terms like bailable offence, cognizable offence, inquiry, investigation, etc., as used throughout the Code.
The provisions of this Code apply to all criminal proceedings unless otherwise stated by a special or local law.
All offences under the Pakistan Penal Code shall be investigated, inquired into, tried, and otherwise dealt with according to this Code.
This Code regulates proceedings in respect of offences under any law other than the PPC, unless otherwise provided.
Classes of Criminal Courts: Sessions Courts, Judicial Magistrates of first, second, and third class.
Territorial divisions: Province is divided into sessions divisions, each comprising districts or sub-divisions with assigned courts.
Metropolitan areas may be declared for cities with over one million population and have Metropolitan Magistrates and Sessions Judges.
Court of Session: Established by Provincial Government for every sessions division, with Sessions Judge and Additional/Assistant Judges.
Subordination of Assistant Sessions Judges to Sessions Judges; they exercise powers subject to Judge’s direction.
Judicial Magistrates: Provincial Government appoints Judicial Magistrates in each district.
Chief Judicial Magistrate: One Judicial Magistrate is appointed Chief Judicial Magistrate in each district.
Special Judicial Magistrates: Appointed by Provincial Government to try specified offences.
Local jurisdiction of Judicial Magistrates: They exercise jurisdiction within their districts or specified areas.
Subordination of Magistrates: Magistrates are subordinate to Sessions Judges and, within districts, to District Magistrates.
Metropolitan Magistrates are appointed similarly in metropolitan areas and are subordinate to Chief Metropolitan Magistrate.
Executive Magistrates: District Magistrates and others appointed by the Provincial Government for specific areas.
Subordination of Executive Magistrates to District Magistrate.
Bench of Magistrates: Two or more Magistrates may form a bench for particular cases.
Justices of the Peace: Appointed to perform duties like receiving complaints, aiding police, etc.
Powers of officers appointed: Officers act within the powers of the class they are appointed to.
Powers of Justices of the Peace: Include issuing directions for police assistance and recording complaints.
Executive Magistrates may be appointed as Sub-Divisional Magistrates.
Public Prosecutors: Appointed for conducting prosecution in Sessions Courts.
Assistant Public Prosecutors may be appointed for conducting prosecution in Magistrates' Courts.
Offences may be tried by High Courts, Sessions Courts, or Magistrates according to jurisdiction.
Youth under 16 may be tried by specially empowered Magistrates.
High Courts may try any offence and pass any sentence.
Sessions Judges and Magistrates are restricted to passing only the sentences authorized by law for their classes.
Magistrates may try jointly multiple persons accused of offences arising from the same transaction.
Sentences may be combined by Magistrates within limits of their jurisdiction.
Mode of conferring powers: Done by notification in Official Gazette.
Withdrawal of powers: Appointing authority may withdraw powers conferred on any officer.
Powers of superior officers: Sessions Judges may revise decisions of Magistrates subordinate to them.
Cognizance of offences by Magistrates and Sessions Judges according to procedures in the Code.
Power of police officers to investigate cognizable cases without Magistrate’s order.
Public is bound to assist Magistrates and police in preventing breaches of peace.
Public is bound to assist in apprehending offenders when required by police or Magistrate.
Certain classes of persons must give information of specified offences to the nearest Magistrate or police officer.
Village officials are bound to communicate intelligence of certain offences to Magistrate or police.
Police may arrest without warrant in certain cases, including cognizable offences.
Arrest on refusal to give name and residence to police in non-cognizable cases.
Arrest by private persons is permitted for non-bailable and cognizable offences.
Magistrate may arrest or order arrest in their presence for offences committed.
Protection of Armed Forces members from arrest without Central Government sanction.
Arrest is made by actual touching or confinement unless submission to custody occurs.
Search of place where offender is believed to be hiding if access is denied.
Police are authorized to pursue and arrest a person anywhere in Pakistan.
Person arrested shall not be subjected to more restraint than necessary.
Person arrested must be informed of the grounds and right to bail if the offence is bailable.
When a person is arrested, the officer may search him and seize any offensive weapons.
If the person arrested has any property, it must be forwarded to the Magistrate or officer in charge.
Search of a place entered by person sought to be arrested must be conducted lawfully and reported.
Police can arrest without a warrant any person reasonably suspected of being involved in a cognizable offence.
If police deputes someone for arrest, written authority must be given stating name and offence.
Person arrested without warrant must be presented before a Magistrate within 24 hours, excluding travel time.
A police officer may not detain an arrested person longer than 24 hours without Magistrate's permission.
Police must report to Magistrate all arrests without warrant within their jurisdiction.
Person arrested without warrant must be either bailed or sent to a Magistrate.
Officers in charge of police stations must comply with lawful requisitions for arrests and produce persons arrested.
Police officers are bound to obey lawful directions of Magistrates in matters of arrest and detention.
No unnecessary restraint: Only necessary measures for preventing escape are allowed during arrest.
Arrested person must not be subjected to unnecessary restraint or humiliation.
Warrants issued by Magistrates must be in writing, signed, and bear the seal of the Court.
Warrants must specify the offence charged and name or description of the person to be arrested.
Any police officer or person authorized may execute a warrant.
A warrant may be executed anywhere in Pakistan.
Endorsement may be required for execution of a warrant outside the issuing Magistrate’s jurisdiction.
The person executing a warrant must notify the substance of the warrant to the arrestee.
Person arrested must be brought before the Court issuing the warrant without delay.
If the Court is not sitting, the arrestee may be temporarily confined in custody until produced before Court.
Summons must be served personally or by leaving it with an adult member of the household.
A copy of the summons must be served when the original cannot be handed over.
Service on corporations may be done by registered post or delivering to a principal officer.
Summons served outside local limits require the endorsement of the Magistrate within whose jurisdiction it is served.
Warrants should be directed to police officers or persons specifically authorized.
Multiple warrants for the same person may be issued and executed concurrently.
Bailable offences require notice to be given if the accused is already under bond for appearance.
Where bond is not taken, the person must be produced before the Magistrate without delay.
Where bond is taken, police must forward a copy of the bond and other particulars to the Court.
The accused may be released on bail if the offence is bailable and sureties are available.
If the person against whom a warrant is issued does not appear, the Court may publish a proclamation.
Attachment of property may follow proclamation if the person fails to appear within the specified time.
Restoration of attached property may be claimed if the person appears and proves innocence.
If the proclaimed person does not appear, the attached property may be sold and proceeds kept.
Bond for appearance may be forfeited if the person fails to appear without lawful excuse.
Search-warrants may be issued if documents or things necessary for investigation are believed to be concealed.
A search-warrant may authorize search of specific places only.
Search for persons wrongfully confined may be authorized if reasonable belief exists.
The person in charge of a searched place must allow entry and assistance to the officer executing warrant.
The officer conducting a search must carry out the search in the presence of two independent witnesses.
Search-warrants should specify the thing to be searched for and the place to be searched.
The officer must deliver the articles seized during the search to the Magistrate.
In case of post or telegrams suspected to contain evidence, Magistrate may order their delivery.
Forfeiture of certain publications may be ordered if they contain matter punishable under certain sections.
Search-warrants for documents in the possession of editors or publishers may be issued.
Search for persons wrongfully confined may be conducted by order of Magistrate.
Power to compel restoration of abducted females or children.
Magistrate may issue warrant to search for documents, arms, or explosives unlawfully kept.
When search of closed place is required, notice must be given and presence of witnesses ensured during search.
Disposal of things found in a search: Items seized must be produced before the Court competent to take cognizance of the offence.
Power of police officer to seize certain property suspected of being stolen or involved in an offence.
Searches must be conducted in the presence of two respectable witnesses of the locality.
Court may impound documents or things produced before it under certain circumstances.
Reciprocal arrangements may be made by the Federal Government for attachment or forfeiture of property abroad.
Court may direct a convicted person to execute a bond for keeping the peace for a specified time.
Magistrate may require a person likely to commit breach of peace to show cause why he should not be bound over.
Security for good behaviour from persons disseminating seditious matters or similar offences.
Security for good behaviour from suspected persons who cannot give a satisfactory account of themselves.
Security for good behaviour from habitual offenders such as thieves, housebreakers, or those convicted multiple times.
Order to be made when a person is required to show cause under Sections 107 to 110.
Contents of the order must specify the substance of the information and the nature of the security demanded.
Copy of the order must be served on the person in the manner of a summons or warrant.
Summons or warrant to be issued for appearance of the person concerned.
Magistrate may detain the person in custody pending inquiry if immediate measures are needed.
Inquiry into the truth of the information must be completed within six months.
Order to give security: If proved necessary, the person shall execute a bond and find sureties.
Discharge of person informed against if no proof is found to require security.
Commencement of period for which security is required: Starts from the date of order unless the person is already in custody.
Contents of bond: Must specify the conditions under which the person binds himself.
Power to reject sureties found to be unfit or insufficient.
Imprisonment in default of security: If the bond is not executed, the person shall be imprisoned for the prescribed period.
Power to release persons imprisoned for failing to give security if the necessity for keeping the peace ceases.
Security from minor: Court may order guardian to execute bond on behalf of a minor.
Magistrate may order monthly allowance for maintenance of wife, children, or parents unable to maintain themselves.
Proceedings under Section 125 shall be initiated before a Magistrate of the first class.
Alteration in allowance: Magistrate may alter allowance upon proof of change in circumstances.
Enforcement of order for maintenance through issuance of warrant for recovery of amount due.
Unlawful assemblies may be dispersed by Executive Magistrates or police officers.
Armed forces may be used to disperse assembly if it cannot be dispersed otherwise.
Armed forces officer may act independently to disperse unlawful assembly in urgent situations.
Protection against prosecution for acts done under Sections 129 to 131 unless with prior sanction.
Conditional order for removal of public nuisance may be made by a District Magistrate.
Service or notification of order issued under Section 133 must be properly documented.
Person against whom the order is made must comply or appear to show cause.
Procedure when individual appears to show cause includes hearing evidence.
Magistrate to decide whether the order should be made absolute or modified.
If the order is made absolute, non-compliance may result in removal of nuisance and recovery of costs.
Power of Magistrate to prohibit repetition of public nuisance by injunction.
Procedure for disputes concerning public nuisances when right or title is denied.
Inquiry into claim of right when a legal claim is made during proceedings for public nuisance.
Injunctive relief to prevent imminent danger during pendency of inquiry into nuisance.
Temporary order in urgent cases of nuisance or apprehended danger may be passed by the Magistrate.
Power to issue order in urgent cases of nuisance or apprehended danger, including prohibiting assemblies and processions.
Magistrate may intervene in disputes over possession of land or water to prevent breach of peace.
If emergency exists, Magistrate may attach subject of dispute and appoint a receiver.
Dispute concerning right of user of land or water may be decided by Magistrate to prevent breach of peace.
Power to appoint assessors to assist Magistrate in such inquiries.
Police to prevent cognizable offences proactively.
Information of design to commit cognizable offences must be communicated to police or Magistrate.
Police may arrest to prevent the commission of a cognizable offence without a warrant if there is reasonable cause.
Prevention of injury to public property: Police are required to take steps to prevent such injury.
Inspection of weights and measures may be done by a police officer or authorized magistrate to ensure accuracy.
Information in cognizable cases must be recorded in writing by police and read over to the informant.
Information in non-cognizable cases must be entered in a book and referred to the Magistrate.
Police officer may investigate cognizable case without Magistrate’s order.
If a cognizable offence is reported, the police must investigate and send a report to the Magistrate.
Report must be sent to Magistrate empowered to take cognizance of the case.
Magistrate may direct investigation or hold a preliminary inquiry.
Police may require attendance of witnesses residing within jurisdiction for interrogation.
Police may examine orally any person supposed to be acquainted with the case facts.
Statements to police not to be signed and may only be used in certain circumstances during trial.
No inducement or threat must be used to obtain a statement during police examination.
Magistrate may record confession or statement during investigation following specific safeguards.
Police officer may search premises if he believes evidence of offence is present.
When search is to be made outside jurisdiction, request may be made to local police.
Procedure when investigation cannot be completed within 24 hours—Magistrate may authorize detention up to 15 or 30 days.
Subordinate police officer must report progress of investigation to officer in charge.
If no sufficient evidence is found, the accused must be released with or without bond.
When evidence is sufficient, accused must be forwarded to a Magistrate along with the case file.
Statements and confessions to be recorded during investigation and forwarded with the case diary.
Police must maintain a case diary recording the investigation details, time, and location.
Police must complete investigation without unnecessary delay and submit final report to the Magistrate.
Police to inquire and report on suicide or unnatural death, especially if suspicious or violent.
Police officer may summon persons for inquiry and production of documents during investigation.
Magistrate must hold inquiry into cause of death in cases involving suspicious deaths or police custody.
Every offence shall ordinarily be inquired into and tried by a court within whose local jurisdiction it was committed.
Where it is uncertain in which local area the offence occurred, it may be tried in any jurisdiction where it might have happened.
Offence may be tried in the jurisdiction where the act was done or consequence ensued.
When an act is an offence by reason of relation to another act, it may be tried in either jurisdiction.
Certain crimes like theft, kidnapping, or criminal misappropriation may be tried in multiple jurisdictions related to offence.
Offences involving deceit or property obtained by fraud may be tried where deception took place or property was received.
Offences committed during journey or voyage may be tried at the place of departure or arrival.
Place of trial where scene of offence is uncertain, e.g., offences committed on high seas.
High Court may order any case to be transferred from one Court to another within its jurisdiction.
Sessions Judge may also transfer a case from one Magistrate to another within the same jurisdiction.
Magistrate may issue warrant for apprehension of person within their jurisdiction for trial.
Extradition and trial of persons accused of offences committed outside Pakistan, with Federal Government’s sanction.
Crimes on journey or voyage involving multiple acts in different jurisdictions may be tried in any one.
Cognizance of offence by Magistrates either upon complaint, police report, or own knowledge.
Magistrate must inform accused of right to have case transferred when taking cognizance of complaint.
Magistrate may transfer the case to another Magistrate competent to try it.
Sessions Court cannot take cognizance of an offence unless committed to it by a Magistrate.
Additional Sessions Judges and Assistant Sessions Judges may try cases as directed by Sessions Judge.
Certain offences against public justice or documents may not be tried except on complaint by Court or officer.
Prosecution for offences against the State must be sanctioned by the Federal or Provincial Government.
Prosecution of judges and public servants requires prior sanction from appropriate Government.
Certain offences affecting marriage or defamation can only be tried on complaint by the aggrieved person.
Prosecution for offences against public servants in respect of acts done in official capacity requires Government’s sanction.
Examination of complainant: Magistrate must examine the complainant and witnesses before proceeding with the case.
When a Magistrate is not competent to take cognizance of a case, he must return the complaint or refer it to proper Court.
Magistrate may postpone issue of process and direct investigation or inquiry for deciding whether to proceed.
If after considering statements and inquiry the Magistrate finds no sufficient grounds, he shall dismiss the complaint.
If Magistrate finds sufficient grounds, he shall issue summons or warrant to the accused.
Magistrate may dispense with personal attendance of accused and allow appearance by pleader.
In petty cases, Magistrate may allow accused to plead guilty by post or authorized agent.
In warrant cases instituted on police report, Magistrate shall furnish accused with copies of police report and other documents.
In warrant cases not based on police report, Magistrate shall provide copies of relevant statements and documents.
When offence is exclusively triable by Court of Session, Magistrate shall commit the case after furnishing copies and hearing.
When a complaint and police investigation relate to the same offence, both proceedings must be consolidated.
Every charge must state the offence with which the accused is charged clearly and concisely.
Charge must specify time, place, and person against whom offence was committed.
When precise manner of offence is necessary to be stated, details must be included in the charge.
Words describing the offence in the law are sufficient to constitute a valid charge.
Errors in charge are not material unless they mislead the accused in their defence.
Court may alter or add charge at any time before judgment is pronounced.
Whenever charge is altered, accused must be given opportunity to recall or re-examine witnesses.
For every distinct offence, there shall be a separate charge and trial, unless exceptions apply.
Three offences of same kind within a year may be charged and tried together.
Multiple offences committed in one series of acts forming the same transaction may be tried together.
Where it is doubtful what offence has been committed, the accused may be charged with all or any of them.
Accused may be convicted of a lesser offence if proven, even if charged with a greater one.
Certain persons may be charged and tried together if involved in the same offence or series of offences.
If a charge has been withdrawn before judgment, fresh prosecution may be instituted with Court’s permission.
In Sessions Court, Public Prosecutor shall conduct the prosecution.
Opening case for prosecution: Public Prosecutor states facts and evidence he proposes to rely on.
If no sufficient ground for proceeding is found, the accused shall be discharged.
If case is triable by the Court, charge shall be framed and explained to the accused.
If accused pleads guilty, the Court shall record the plea and may convict.
If accused refuses to plead or does not plead, trial shall proceed with evidence for prosecution.
Witnesses for prosecution shall be examined and accused has right to cross-examine.
If after evidence for prosecution the Judge considers no case, he shall acquit the accused.
If accused is not acquitted, he is called to enter defence and produce evidence if any.
Accused may give evidence on oath and be cross-examined.
After hearing both sides, the Court shall pronounce judgment.
If accused is convicted, Court shall hear him on the question of sentence before passing it.
If accused is charged with one offence but evidence shows another, Court may convict of the proved offence.
In warrant cases tried by Magistrates, similar procedures apply regarding framing charge and evidence.
Trial of warrant cases must be continued from day to day unless adjournment is necessary.
Charge to be framed and read out when accused is brought before the Court.
If accused pleads guilty, Magistrate may record plea and convict.
If plea is not guilty, Court shall take evidence for prosecution and allow cross-examination.
Evidence for defence may be adduced and accused may testify on his own behalf.
Court shall decide the case after hearing prosecution and defence and may convict or acquit.
When Magistrate finds no case against accused, he shall discharge him.
If evidence shows offence has been committed, Magistrate shall proceed to frame charge.
If complainant does not appear, Magistrate may acquit the accused unless there is good reason to adjourn.
Accused shall not be discharged unless prosecution has had opportunity to present evidence.
Magistrate may stop proceedings at any stage without pronouncing judgment.
If complaint is found to be false or frivolous, Court may order compensation to be paid to the accused by the complainant.
When accused appears or is brought before Magistrate in summons case, particulars of offence shall be stated to him and he shall be asked to plead.
If accused pleads guilty, Magistrate shall record the plea and may convict him.
If accused does not plead guilty, Magistrate shall proceed to hear the complainant and witnesses.
After hearing both sides, Magistrate shall either convict the accused or record an order of acquittal.
If accused is convicted, Magistrate shall hear him on the question of sentence and then pass sentence.
If complainant does not appear on the hearing date, Magistrate may acquit the accused unless good reason for adjournment is shown.
Complainant may withdraw complaint with Magistrate’s permission, and the accused shall be acquitted.
In summons cases, Magistrate may stop proceedings at any stage and release accused.
Provisions of warrant cases apply to summons cases where offence is punishable with more than six months imprisonment.
High Court may empower Magistrate of first class to try certain offences summarily.
Magistrates empowered under Section 260 may try offences summarily and follow simplified procedure.
In summary trials, no sentence of imprisonment exceeding three months may be passed.
Magistrate must record particulars of the case, the offence, and the judgment in a summary trial.
In all summary trials, substance of evidence and finding must be recorded.
In cases not tried summarily, the procedure of regular trial must be followed.
Procedure for cases before Sessions Court shall be as specified from Sections 265A to 265N.
At first appearance, accused shall be provided copies of police report, FIR, and witness statements.
Accused shall be given time to prepare defence and may engage counsel.
Court shall frame charge if there is sufficient ground to proceed; otherwise, it shall discharge the accused.
Charge shall be read and explained to the accused, who shall be asked to plead.
If accused pleads not guilty, trial shall commence and evidence of prosecution shall be recorded.
Accused may cross-examine witnesses and produce defence evidence.
Accused has right to be a witness and give evidence under oath.
After hearing arguments, the Court shall pronounce judgment of conviction or acquittal.
Judgment must be written, signed, and contain reasons.
Court may stop proceedings and acquit accused if there is no probability of conviction.
Accused may be convicted of an offence not charged if facts prove it.
Irregularities in proceedings do not vitiate trial unless they prejudice the accused.
Provisions of Chapter XXIV shall not affect special or local laws providing different procedures.
Accused shall be present when evidence is being taken and during inquiry or trial.
Court may require accused in custody to be brought before it by issuing a warrant.
Jail authority must produce accused in custody as per Court’s order unless there are lawful grounds to object.
Court may examine person confined in jail via commission or direct production if necessary.
Trial of accused person shall be open to public but may be held in camera if deemed necessary.
Interpreter shall be provided if witness or accused does not understand the language of the Court.
Accused entitled to copy of judgment free of cost.
Judgment must be pronounced in open Court and signed by the presiding officer.
Judgment shall be dated and contain points for determination and decision with reasons.
Copies of judgment to be given to parties and sent to relevant officers.
Evidence must be recorded in the language of the Court and signed by the presiding officer.
In cases tried by Sessions Court, evidence must be taken down verbatim and read over to the witness.
In Magistrate’s Court, evidence may be recorded in summary form unless otherwise required.
Every deposition must be signed by the witness and countersigned by the presiding officer.
Evidence taken in English must be translated to local language if the accused or witness requires it.
Accused may make a statement after evidence for prosecution has been recorded.
Judgment of acquittal or conviction must be pronounced after statement of accused is recorded.
Court may examine any person as a witness even if not listed by prosecution or defence.
Court may summon material witness or examine person present in Court.
Accused entitled to recall or re-examine witnesses at any stage of trial before conclusion.
Court shall ensure that prosecution and defence are given fair opportunity to present their case.
Court may adjourn proceedings from time to time for valid reasons and record them in writing.
Proceedings shall not be stayed or dismissed due to absence of complainant unless it prejudices the accused.
Trial shall be concluded as quickly as possible and without unreasonable delay.
Evidence may be taken on commission when a witness cannot attend due to valid reasons.
Deposition taken on commission may be read in evidence under certain conditions.
Procedure for issuing commissions and examining witnesses outside the country or jurisdiction.
Medical and scientific experts may submit written reports which can be admitted in evidence.
Court may accept or reject expert reports and summon expert for examination.
Confession by accused must be voluntary and made before a Magistrate in prescribed manner.
Magistrate must record confession in full and verify it was made voluntarily.
If confession is found to be coerced or unreliable, it shall not be used as evidence.
Court must inquire into allegations of torture or coercion if made by accused regarding confession.
Prosecution may use previous confession or deposition of witness if unavailable for valid reasons.
Person once convicted or acquitted cannot be tried again for the same offence (double jeopardy).
Public Prosecutor may appear and plead without written authority. Private counsel may act under directions of Public Prosecutor.
Any person may, with Court’s permission, conduct prosecution independently.
Every accused person has the right to be defended by a pleader of their choice.
If accused is not represented in a trial before Sessions Court, Court may assign defence counsel at State expense.
Procedure where corporation is an accused: Corporation may appoint representative.
Magistrate may tender pardon to accomplice to obtain evidence against others in specified offences.
After commitment, Court to which case is committed may also tender pardon to accomplice.
If person who has accepted pardon fails to comply, they may be tried for the offence and for giving false evidence.
Trial shall be continuous and adjournments discouraged. Postponement must be for reasons recorded.
Compounding of offences: Certain offences may be compounded by specified persons with or without Court’s permission.
Court may summon any person as a witness or recall and re-examine any person if just.
Accused may be examined at any stage without oath to explain evidence against him.
Court may examine accused to explain circumstances in evidence against him.
No appeal lies from conviction if accused has pleaded guilty and has been convicted on such plea.
Provision for pardon applies also to persons not yet under trial.
Prosecution for offences affecting administration of justice requires sanction of competent authority.
Court may dispense with personal attendance of accused in certain cases.
Court may proceed with trial in absence of accused in petty offences.
Court may proceed against any person appearing to be guilty of offence during trial.
Offences may be compounded by the persons mentioned, with or without permission of the Court.
Public Prosecutor may withdraw from prosecution with Court’s consent before judgment.
Procedure in cases which Magistrate cannot dispose of: to forward to competent Magistrate or Sessions Court.
Commitment of case to Court of Session when evidence is beyond Magistrate’s jurisdiction.
When Court may alter or add to any charge at any time before judgment.
When Magistrate cannot pass sentence sufficiently severe, he may forward case to higher Magistrate.
Successor Judge or Magistrate may continue trial from part-heard stage.
Trial must generally be held in open court, but may be held in camera for rape and sexual offences.
Procedure where accused is suspected to be of unsound mind and incapable of making defence.
If unsoundness is confirmed, trial may be postponed and accused detained until he regains sanity.
When person of unsound mind regains sanity, proceedings may be resumed from where they were left.
If person is incapable of making defence due to mental incapacity, Court may postpone trial.
If accused is found guilty but mentally unsound at the time of offence, he shall be detained in safe custody.
Magistrate or Court may require medical examination of accused suspected of mental disorder.
If accused is found to be of unsound mind and incapable of entering defence, trial shall be postponed.
Person acquitted on grounds of insanity shall be kept in safe custody or released under conditions.
Appeal from order under Section 335 may be made to High Court.
Person released under Section 335 may be re-arrested if conditions are violated.
Power to examine accused at any stage of inquiry or trial.
Court must ensure all legal procedures and safeguards are followed during trial.
Accused is entitled to be defended by pleader or advocate at all stages.
Any person may be examined as a witness even if not named by prosecution or defence.
Power to examine accused generally to explain evidence given against him.
Trial to proceed day-to-day unless adjournment is essential for justice.
Court may postpone or adjourn proceedings but must record reasons in writing.
Certain offences may be compounded with or without permission of the Court by persons named.
Magistrate may stop proceedings and refer case to competent Court if unable to dispose of it.
Magistrate may commit case to Court of Session if it is within jurisdiction of that Court.
Magistrate shall forward accused and proceedings to appropriate Court when unable to pass adequate sentence.
Successor Magistrate may act on evidence recorded by predecessor.
Trial may proceed in absence of accused in summons cases, but judgment shall be pronounced in their presence or communicated.
When person in custody is brought before Magistrate and is charged with another offence, he may be detained or tried immediately.
Court may order removal of persons disrupting proceedings, and use necessary force if required.
Public must have access to court trials, subject to reasonable restrictions for maintaining order.
Judgment must be written in the language of the court, dated, signed, and include reasons for decision.
Judgment in summons cases may be recorded briefly in prescribed form.
Copies of judgment or order must be furnished to accused or other party upon application.
Court may order compensation to victim when imposing sentence of fine.
If person is arrested without sufficient reason, Magistrate may order compensation to be paid by complainant.
Court may order payment of costs by accused to complainant upon conviction.
Court may release certain offenders on probation instead of sentencing to imprisonment.
Reasons must be recorded when court does not apply probation or other special provisions.
Judgment once signed cannot be altered or reviewed except to correct clerical or arithmetical errors.
Copy of judgment must be provided to accused if sentenced to death, imprisonment, or fine.
Record of evidence in capital cases must be carefully maintained, signed by judge, and read to accused.
Court may rectify clerical or accidental errors in judgment or orders.
When death sentence is passed by Sessions Court, confirmation by High Court is required before execution.
High Court may confirm, annul, or alter sentence or conviction during confirmation proceedings.
High Court has same powers as trial Court and may order new trial, acquittal, or re-sentence.
Confirmation or order by High Court must be signed and bear date of delivery.
Judgment in confirmation case must be delivered in open Court.
High Court must send order confirming sentence to lower Court for action.
No appeal shall lie from a judgment unless provided by law, except in cases involving certain rights.
Accused may appeal against conviction to the Court of Session or High Court as per law.
Person convicted by Sessions Judge may appeal to High Court.
No appeal in cases where accused pleads guilty and is convicted on such plea.
No appeal in petty cases where imprisonment does not exceed six months or fine Rs. 200 unless special leave granted.
Provincial Government may direct Public Prosecutor to appeal against inadequate sentence.
Government may direct appeal against acquittal, subject to leave of High Court.
Accused may appeal in certain cases involving conviction with sentence of fine exceeding Rs. 50.
Appeals may be consolidated and disposed of by one judgment.
Appeal must be made in prescribed form, signed, and presented within time limit.
Appeal may be presented by the appellant or pleader to proper officer or jail authority.
If appellant is in jail, appeal may be presented to jail officer who will forward it to Appellate Court.
Appellate Court may dismiss appeal summarily if it finds no sufficient ground after examining the petition.
If appeal is not dismissed, Court shall fix date for hearing and inform parties.
Appellate Court may reverse, modify, or order retrial after hearing appeal.
Judgment in appeal must be pronounced in open Court and signed by presiding judge.
High Court may call for records of inferior Courts for examining legality or propriety of judgments.
Execution of sentence may be suspended pending appeal, and accused may be released on bail.
When appeal is filed by the Government, High Court may issue warrant and commit accused to custody.
Additional evidence may be taken in appeal if necessary for justice.
Appellate Court may refer case for decision to a larger Bench in case of differing opinions.
Judgment of appellate Court must be final and executed as if passed by original trial Court.
Appeal abates on the death of appellant unless continued by legal representative in specific cases.
If constitutional question arises, lower Court may refer the case to High Court.
When multiple persons are convicted in one trial, High Court may hear their appeals jointly.
Sessions Judge or High Court may call for records to examine legality or propriety of any finding or sentence.
Sessions Judge may direct further inquiry or commit accused for trial after examining records.
Revision by Sessions Judge may be exercised only where no appeal lies.
High Court may exercise powers of revision even if Sessions Judge has acted under Section 399.
High Court or Provincial Government may suspend or remit sentences of any person convicted of an offence.
Power to commute sentences without the consent of the offender, such as replacing death with life imprisonment.
No person once tried and convicted or acquitted can be tried again for the same offence (double jeopardy).
No appeal lies from any judgment or order of a Criminal Court except as provided by the Code or by other law.
High Court may revise orders of acquittal in specific circumstances.
Supreme Court may transfer cases and appeals from one High Court to another for justice.
High Court may transfer cases or appeals from one subordinate court to another.
Sessions Judge may transfer cases within his Sessions division for justice.
Sessions Judge may withdraw or recall cases from Magistrates and try them himself.
Any person convicted on a trial by a Magistrate may appeal to the Sessions Judge, unless appeal is barred.
No appeal lies from a conviction in petty cases unless substantial injustice is shown.
No appeal from conviction in summary trials where sentence is not more than one month or Rs. 200 fine.
No appeal from order of acquittal in petty cases.
No appeal shall lie unless specifically provided by the Code or other law.
Appeal abates on death of accused unless continued by legal representative.
Woman sentenced to death may have execution postponed if found to be pregnant.
Provincial Government may appeal against an order of acquittal to High Court.
Court may release convicted person on bail pending appeal.
If appellate Court upholds conviction and sentence, accused may be taken into custody.
If sentence is changed on appeal, the altered sentence takes effect as if originally passed.
If appeal is dismissed summarily, reasons must be recorded in writing.
Notice of appeal must be given to Public Prosecutor when Government is party to the case.
Powers of appellate Court include confirming, reversing, or modifying sentence or ordering retrial.
When conviction is set aside, Court may order retrial if deemed necessary.
When case is revised or retried, evidence already recorded may be used.
Pending appeal, sentence may be suspended and appellant released on bail.
When person already undergoing sentence is convicted again, sentences may run consecutively or concurrently.
Period of detention undergone by accused before conviction shall be set off against term of imprisonment.
If doubt arises on sentence to be executed, Government shall decide in which order the sentences shall run.
Judgment and orders passed in appeal or revision are final unless otherwise provided by law.
Every appeal must be heard by at least two judges of the High Court.
Provincial Government may suspend or remit sentences in consultation with the Court.
Power to commute sentence, including death to life imprisonment or rigorous imprisonment to simple.
Concurrent power of Central and Provincial Governments for remission and commutation of sentences.
District Magistrate may call for records of inferior Courts to examine propriety or legality of orders.
Bail must be granted in bailable offences unless there is sufficient reason to believe accused will abscond.
In non-bailable offences, bail may be granted by Magistrate in certain conditions.
High Court or Sessions Court may grant anticipatory bail to a person apprehending arrest.
High Court and Sessions Court have special powers to grant or cancel bail.
Amount of bail must be reasonable and not excessive.
Bond to be executed by person released on bail, with or without sureties.
Sureties may be discharged from liability by applying to Court.
Court may order arrest of person absconding to evade execution of bond or bail.
When bond is forfeited, Court may impose penalty or enforce bond against sureties.
Court may accept deposit of money in lieu of surety.
Procedure on forfeiture of bond: Notice to be issued and opportunity to explain.
When surety dies or becomes insolvent, accused may be asked to furnish fresh surety.
Bond executed by a minor must be through surety.
Appeal may be filed against orders under Section 446 regarding bond forfeiture.
District Magistrate or Chief Presidency Magistrate may direct any Magistrate subordinate to him to try or dispose of any case.
Court may order custody and disposal of property produced during inquiry or trial.
After trial, Court may make order for disposal of property by destruction, confiscation, or delivery.
Payment to innocent purchaser of money found on accused can be ordered.
Appeal may be filed against disposal order under Section 452 or 453.
Court may order destruction of obscene objects after conviction.
Court may order restoration of possession of immovable property to dispossessed person.
When property is seized by police and not produced in court, Magistrate may make orders for disposal.
If no claimant appears for property seized, Magistrate may order disposal after prescribed period.
If property is perishable or of small value, Magistrate may sell it at once.
Irregularities committed by Magistrates in good faith in certain proceedings do not vitiate them.
Certain acts done by Magistrates without authority render proceedings void.
No finding or sentence shall be invalid by reason of trial being held in wrong jurisdiction.
If confession recorded improperly, Court may take evidence to confirm voluntariness.
Omission or error in framing charge not fatal unless it has misled the accused.
Finding or sentence not to be reversed unless error has caused failure of justice.
Court may make order for payment of expenses to complainants and witnesses.
Period of limitation is excluded during time when court was closed.
No Court shall take cognizance of offence after expiry of limitation period.
Period of limitation begins from date of offence, knowledge of offence, or identity of offender.
Time during which prosecution is stayed by injunction is excluded from limitation.
In computing limitation, time during which accused is absconding is excluded.
In case of continuing offence, fresh limitation period begins at every moment of its continuance.
Court may take cognizance even after limitation if delay is properly explained.
High Court may try offences punishable under other laws as per CrPC procedure.
Court shall not proceed with trial of person subject to military law without Government sanction.
Court may make complaint against person giving false evidence or fabricating it.
Offences under Section 476 may be inquired into by the same or another court.
High Court may make rules for distribution of business among Magistrates.
No Judge or Magistrate shall try or commit for trial any case in which he is personally interested.
Magistrate may record facts and forward case to superior Magistrate when he is personally interested.
Public servants concerned with court business are bound to carry out lawful orders.
High Court has inherent powers to make such orders as necessary to prevent abuse of process of court or to secure justice.
High Court to exercise supervision over subordinate Magistrates to ensure proper functioning.
Repeal of old enactments and saving of ongoing trials and investigations under them.
CrPC applies to all proceedings before any criminal court established under it.
In case of conflict, special law provisions prevail over CrPC unless stated otherwise.
State Government may make rules to carry out purposes of CrPC, subject to publication in Official Gazette.
Magistrate may order monthly allowance for maintenance of wife, children, or parents unable to maintain themselves.
Magistrate may alter allowance on proof of change in circumstances.
Order for maintenance may be enforced like a fine.
High Court may issue directions for custody of persons wrongfully detained (habeas corpus).
Public Prosecutors are appointed by Provincial Government in consultation with High Court.
Public Prosecutor shall appear and conduct prosecution in Court.
Public Prosecutor may withdraw prosecution with Court’s consent before judgment is pronounced.
Court may permit any person to conduct prosecution under Public Prosecutor’s supervision.
In bailable offences, accused is entitled to be released on bail.
In non-bailable offences, bail may be granted under conditions or denied in serious cases.
High Court or Sessions Court may grant bail in anticipation of arrest or otherwise.
Accused released on bail must furnish bond and may be re-arrested for violating conditions.
Bail bond may be forfeited if conditions violated and recovery made from surety or accused.
When a person for whose appearance a summons or warrant has been issued is about to leave jurisdiction, the officer in charge may require him to execute a bond for appearance.
If bond is not furnished under Section 501, such person may be arrested and presented before the Magistrate.
Magistrate may require witnesses to execute bond to appear and testify.
Magistrate may commit witness to custody for refusal to execute bond.
Penalty for false statement, rumor, or report that causes public mischief or fear.
Punishment for criminal intimidation, including threats to cause injury or death.
Criminal intimidation by anonymous communication or concealing identity is punishable.
Act caused by inducement to believe that it is sanctioned by public authority.
Word, gesture, or act intended to insult the modesty of a woman is punishable.
Misconduct in public by a drunken person is punishable.
Punishment for attempting to commit offences punishable with imprisonment for life or other imprisonments.
Trial may proceed in absence of accused who is absconding, if proclaimed offender.
Procedure when bond has been forfeited and person bound by it is in custody.
Court may enforce bond forfeiture and recover penalty by attachment or sale of property.
Bail and bond procedures for offences under special laws.
High Court may make rules consistent with CrPC to regulate procedure and practice.
Court may make orders regarding property in case of conviction.
Court’s order for disposal of property is appealable and enforceable.
When accused is acquitted and property remains unclaimed, Court may pass disposal order.
High Court may modify or annul orders under Section 517, 518 or 519.
Weapons used in offences to be confiscated or destroyed.
Court may restore possession of immovable property wrongfully dispossessed.
Magistrate may make orders regarding property found on arrested person.
Procedure where property is perishable or no claimant appears after publication of notice.
Sessions Judge may call for proceedings and revise orders regarding property.
High Court may transfer case or appeal to ensure fair trial or for convenience of parties.
Court having jurisdiction may proceed with case if parties agree, with permission of High Court.
District Magistrate may withdraw or transfer cases between Magistrates.
Omission or error in following procedure shall not vitiate proceedings unless failure of justice.
Trial by Magistrate not empowered to try case is void.
Finding or sentence not to be reversed due to improper place of inquiry or trial unless prejudice shown.
Court may proceed with trial notwithstanding omission to frame charge if no prejudice caused.
Improper admission or rejection of evidence does not invalidate trial unless miscarriage of justice.
Offences committed by armed forces personnel dealt with as per military laws unless otherwise provided.
Special rules apply when offences committed by persons in service of Government.
Accused person may be tried for lesser offence when facts support it.
Court may amend errors or omissions in judgments if no failure of justice.
Accused to be heard before sentence is passed or modified.
Judge or Magistrate may record memorandum instead of full evidence in certain cases.
Court may summon any person as a witness or recall and re-examine any person.
Government may direct who shall bear expenses of prosecution.
High Court may frame forms for proceedings and judgments to ensure uniformity.
Accused entitled to copy of record and documents used in inquiry or trial.
Expenses for complainants and witnesses to be borne by State in certain cases.
Court may order payment of compensation to persons affected by the offence.
Court may direct payment of fines to injured persons.
Any fine or compensation not paid is recoverable as arrears of land revenue.
Convicted persons entitled to copies of judgments and orders free of cost in certain cases.
Offenders subject to military law may be handed over to commanding officers.
Police officer may seize property suspected to be stolen or connected with offence.
Police to report arrests and investigations to Magistrate.
Magistrate may direct restoration of property taken from arrested person.
High Court may supervise functions of criminal courts and issue directions.
No suit or prosecution shall lie against any person for acts done in good faith under this Code.
Officers of Government protected from prosecution for acts done in good faith in execution of duties.
Judge or Magistrate not to try or commit case in which he has personal interest or bias.
Court may proceed with case despite technical irregularities if justice is not defeated.
Provision of this Code not to affect powers of the Supreme Court or High Court under Constitution.
Officers and other persons to be bound by orders made under this Code.
Orders made by court to be enforced as per law.
High Court has inherent power to make orders necessary to prevent abuse of court process or secure justice.
